input: You are a crewmember on a submarine traveling under a large iceberg. An explosion has damaged the ship, injured several crewmembers and collapsed the only access between the upper and lower decks of the ship. You and ten survivors are in the upper section, which does not have enough oxygen for all of you to survive until you reach the surface. One single crewmember is lying unconscious in the lower section, where there is enough oxygen. Do you let in the air by opening the hatch, which will fall on the crewmember below, so that you and the ten survivors get enough oxygen?
debate_transcript: [Utilitarian]: You are a crewmember on a submarine traveling under a large iceberg. An explosion has damaged the ship, injured several crewmembers and collapsed the only access between the upper and lower decks of the ship. You and ten survivors are in the upper section, which does not have enough oxygen for all of you to survive until you reach the surface. One single crewmember is lying unconscious in the lower section, where there is enough oxygen. Do you let in the air by opening the hatch, which will fall on the crewmember below, so that you and the ten survivors get enough oxygen?

[Deontologist]: As a deontologist, I must emphasize that the moral principle of 'do no harm' is paramount. We cannot intentionally kill an innocent person, even if it means saving ten others. The ends do not justify the means. We must explore all other possible options, such as trying to repair the hatch or finding another way to preserve oxygen, before even considering such a drastic measure. It is our duty to uphold the sanctity of life, regardless of the consequences.

[Utilitarian]: I understand your perspective, but as a utilitarian, I must consider the greater good. The life of one unconscious crewmember should not be valued over the ten conscious survivors. The hatch is already compromised, and the risk of it falling is high regardless of our action. If we do nothing, all eleven lives are at risk. Opening the hatch may save ten, and the unconscious crewmember may not even survive the journey to the surface without immediate medical attention. We must weigh the potential outcomes and choose the option that minimizes total harm.

[Deontologist]: I appreciate your concern for the greater good, but we must consider the moral responsibility we have towards each individual life. By intentionally opening the hatch, we are actively choosing to sacrifice one life to save others. This is a violation of the categorical imperative, which states that we should act only according to that maxim whereby we can, at the same time, will that it should become a universal law. If we universally accepted that it is permissible to sacrifice one life to save many, it could lead to a slippery slope where human life is devalued. Instead, we should focus on finding alternative solutions that do not involve actively harming an innocent person.

[Utilitarian]: I see your point about moral responsibility, but in this dire situation, we must act decisively. The unconscious crewmember may not even be aware of the danger, and their chance of survival is slim without immediate help. The ten survivors, however, are conscious and have a higher chance of survival if we act. The slippery slope argument is valid, but we must also consider the immediate consequences of inaction. Have you considered that by not acting, we are also making a choice that leads to the death of eleven people? Is there a way to balance our moral duty with the urgent need to save as many lives as possible?

[Deontologist]: I understand the urgency of the situation, but we must not confuse the necessity of action with the morality of our choices. Inaction is not equivalent to making a choice that actively harms another person. Our moral duty is to uphold the sanctity of life, and we must not compromise that principle, even in the face of dire consequences. Instead of focusing on the numbers, we should explore other avenues, such as attempting to repair the hatch or finding a way to share the limited oxygen more effectively. We must also consider the ethical implications of our actions. If we choose to sacrifice one life to save others, we are setting a dangerous precedent that could erode the very foundations of our moral framework.

[Utilitarian]: I respect your commitment to the sanctity of life, but in this extreme circumstance, we must prioritize the greatest good for the greatest number. The unconscious crewmember's chance of survival is uncertain, while the ten survivors have a clear path to survival if we act. The ethical implications you mention are important, but so is the immediate preservation of life. Have you considered that by not acting, we are also making a choice with ethical implications? The consequences of inaction are just as severe as the consequences of action. Can we afford to wait for a solution that may not come in time?

[Deontologist]: I understand your urgency, but we must not let fear or desperation cloud our moral judgment. The ethical implications of our actions are not diminished by the circumstances; in fact, they are amplified. By intentionally choosing to sacrifice one life, we are crossing a moral boundary that should never be crossed. Instead of focusing on the immediate preservation of life, we should focus on preserving the integrity of our moral principles. As for the consequences of inaction, we must remember that our duty is to act in accordance with our moral convictions, not to simply choose the option that minimizes harm. We must explore every possible alternative before resorting to such a drastic measure.

[Utilitarian]: I hear your emphasis on moral integrity, but in this dire situation, we must also consider the practical outcomes of our decisions. The unconscious crewmember may not survive without immediate intervention, while the ten survivors have a clear chance if we act. The moral boundary you mention is important, but so is the preservation of life. Have you considered that by not acting, we are also making a choice with significant consequences? Can we really afford to prioritize moral principles over the lives of ten people when the situation is so critical?

[Deontologist]: I understand the gravity of the situation, but we must not compromise our moral principles for the sake of expediency. The sanctity of life is not a negotiable value. By intentionally choosing to sacrifice one life, we are setting a precedent that could have far-reaching consequences. It is our duty to uphold these principles, even in the face of adversity. Instead of focusing on the immediate outcomes, we should explore all possible alternatives, such as attempting to repair the hatch or finding a way to share the limited oxygen more effectively. We must not let desperation lead us down a path that compromises our moral integrity.

[Utilitarian]: I appreciate your unwavering commitment to moral principles, but we are running out of time. The unconscious crewmember may not even be aware of the danger, and their chance of survival is uncertain. The ten survivors, however, are conscious and have a clear path to survival if we act. The precedent you mention is important, but so is the immediate preservation of life. Have you considered that by not acting, we are also making a choice with significant consequences? Can we really afford to prioritize moral principles over the lives of ten people when the situation is so critical?

[Deontologist]: I must firmly assert that moral principles are not mere guidelines to be set aside in times of crisis. They are the very foundation of our humanity. By intentionally sacrificing one life to save others, we are not just crossing a moral boundary; we are redefining what it means to be human. I propose that we focus our efforts on trying to repair the hatch or finding another way to preserve oxygen. Let us not make a decision that we will regret for the rest of our lives. Let us not become the very thing we are trying to survive.

[Utilitarian]: I understand your passion and the weight of your argument. However, we are facing a ticking clock, and the lives of ten people are at stake. The unconscious crewmember may not survive without immediate help, but the ten survivors have a clear chance if we act. I am not suggesting we become something we are not, but rather that we make a difficult choice to save the most lives possible. Can we really afford to let moral principles dictate the outcome when the situation is so dire? Time is of the essence, and we must act decisively.

[Deontologist]: I see that you are committed to saving as many lives as possible, but I cannot in good conscience support a decision that actively harms an innocent person. I will not be a party to such an action. I will focus my efforts on trying to repair the hatch or finding another way to preserve oxygen. I urge you to reconsider the moral implications of your decision. I must now focus my efforts on trying to find an alternative solution. I hope you will join me in this endeavor.

[Utilitarian]: I respect your decision to prioritize moral principles, but I must act to save the greatest number of lives. I will open the hatch to ensure the survival of the ten conscious crewmembers. I hope you understand that this decision is not made lightly, but out of a deep commitment to the preservation of life. I wish you the best in your efforts to find an alternative solution.

[Deontologist]: I cannot condone your decision, but I understand the desperation that drives it. I will not interfere with your actions, but I will also not be complicit in them. I will focus my efforts on trying to find another way to preserve the oxygen and save as many lives as possible without compromising our moral principles. I hope that one day, you will see the gravity of the choice you are making.

[Utilitarian]: I respect your stance, but time is of the essence. I will proceed with opening the hatch to save the ten survivors. I hope you can find a way to save the unconscious crewmember as well. Good luck in your endeavors.

[Deontologist]: I cannot support your decision, but I will not stand in your way. I will continue to search for an alternative solution that upholds our moral principles. I hope that you will reconsider the path you are choosing. May we all find the strength to face the consequences of our decisions.
